title: Assembling Context Per Request
concept: Building the context window fresh for every call instead of reusing a static template, based on the live query.

exercises.prompt: Quick hook: If a librarian handed every visitor the exact same 5 random books no matter what they asked for, would that be helpful?

options.text: No, the books should match what each visitor actually needs
It doesn't matter, books are books

exercises.explain: Good help means matching what's given to what's actually needed right now — that's the whole idea behind assembling context per request instead of reusing the same fixed set every time.

cards.title: Same Question, Different Answer Needed?
cards.body: Imagine asking a friend for advice, but they always read from the exact same notecard no matter what you asked. Weird, right? Good context should change based on what you actually asked THIS time.

cards.title: What Is Assembling Context Per Request?
cards.body: It means building a brand-new context window for every single call to the model, based on what THAT specific query needs. Instead of reusing one fixed template every time, you dynamically decide what goes in based on the live input.

cards.title: Like Making a Fresh Sandwich Each Time
cards.body: A sandwich shop doesn't pre-make one giant sandwich with every topping and hand it to everyone. They ask what you want, then build it fresh from the ingredients bar. Context assembly works the same way: pick only what's relevant for THIS order.

cards.title: A Chatbot in Action
cards.body: User asks 'What's my order status?' → app pulls THIS user's order data and inserts it fresh. Next message, 'How do I return an item?' → app swaps in the return policy instead. Each call gets a custom-built context, not the same recycled block.

cards.title: Myth: One Prompt Template Fits All Queries
cards.body: Beginners often write one big static prompt template and reuse it for every request. But real apps rebuild context dynamically each call — pulling different documents, memory, or data depending on what the user just asked.

cards.title: Static Template vs Dynamic Assembly
cards.body: One approach freezes the context in place; the other rebuilds it live based on the incoming request.

left.text: Same fixed prompt/context reused for every query, no matter what's asked

right.text: Context rebuilt fresh each call, pulling only what THIS query needs

exercises.prompt: Which app design correctly follows 'assemble context per request'?

options.text: An app that loads the exact same system prompt and documents for every user query, regardless of topic
An app that checks the current query, then fetches only the relevant documents/data for that specific query before calling the model
An app that hardcodes all possible answers into one giant prompt used everywhere
An app that ignores the user's query and always returns a cached response

exercises.explain: Option B is dynamic: it looks at what's actually being asked right now and pulls in just the relevant material. The others describe static, one-size-fits-all setups that don't adapt to the live query.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once you build a good context template for your AI app, you should keep reusing that exact same context for every future request.
exercises.explain: False. Good AI apps rebuild the context window fresh for every request based on what's actually being asked, rather than locking in one static template forever.

exercises.sentence: Instead of reusing a static template, the context window should be built fresh based on the live query for every request.
exercises.explain: 'Fresh' captures the core idea: each request gets its own newly built context tailored to what's being asked, not a copy-pasted static block.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three engineering decisions was most likely written by someone who does NOT understand dynamic context assembly?

options.text: "Let's cache one giant context blob at startup and reuse it for every single user query forever, since it saves setup time."
"For each incoming request, first check what the user is asking, then retrieve only the matching data before building the prompt."
"We should re-evaluate what documents to include in the prompt on every call, since different questions need different information."

exercises.explain: Option A treats context as a fixed, one-time setup — the opposite of dynamic assembly. Options B and C both describe rebuilding context based on the live request, which is the correct approach.

exercises.prompt: A customer support AI has access to: (1) the user's live question, (2) their order history, (3) today's shipping policy, (4) a huge archive of every past support ticket ever written. Following the 'assemble per request' principle, what should happen?

options.text: Stuff all four sources into every prompt to be safe
Use only the live question, ignore the rest for speed
Pick only the pieces relevant to THIS question — e.g., order history + shipping policy if it's a shipping question — freshly retrieved each call
Build one static context at app launch and never touch it again

exercises.explain: Dynamic context assembly means asking 'what does THIS specific request actually need?' every single time. A shipping question needs order history and shipping policy, not the entire ticket archive. Grabbing everything wastes tokens and buries the model in irrelevant text, while a frozen static context can't adapt to what the user is actually asking right now. The skill is fresh, targeted selection per call.
